"If I take the deal, where's the gap between what Numeric ships today and what my auditors will want in Q4 2026 for the EU AI Act Annex III 4 high-risk-audit-customer-ready posture — and what's the multi-quarter roadmap to close it?"

Why this matters for finance-ops mid-market buyers under EU AI Act

The combined regulatory pressure from EU AI Act + GDPR + ISO 42001 + ISO 27001 + SOC 2 Type II + ASC 606 + IFRS 15 + Wayfair-economic-nexus + SEC 17a-4 WORM + SOX 404 has made the close-of-record-AI-pipeline a top-3 vendor-DD scrutiny zone for any public-company + regulated-entity + EU-revenue-above-€15M-ARR + multi-entity SaaS-mid-market-accounting-team. Numeric sits at the intersection of every one of these: it's a cloud-native, AI-driven, audit-evidence-heavy platform that finance-ops buyers will vet harder than almost any other SaaS contract in the stack. The companies that get the audit-target-gap matrix right — showing the per-AI-decision 20-column join-table + the per-close-package-bind-event-immutability-evidence + the per-ASC-606-SSP-decision + per-Wayfair-nexus-decision + per-multi-entity-elimination-decision + per-EU-AI-Act-Art.53(d)-training-data-transparency evidence — close at 2-3x the ACV of the ones that don't.

For a privacy officer or security architect evaluating Numeric under EU AI Act + ISO 42001 + IFRS reporting + ASC 606 compliance + Wayfair-economic-nexus sales-tax compliance + multi-entity consolidation timelines, the diligence playbook above becomes a 14-question conversation that maps the audit-target-gap matrix to: (a) AI-decision reasoning-chain provenance depth, (b) ASC 606 + IFRS 15 + Wayfair-economic-nexus + close-package-bind-event decision-log coverage, (c) EU AI Act Art. 53(d) GPAI training-data-transparency disclosure, (d) cross-tenant Numeric SaaS + multi-entity + close-package-bind-event isolation-evidence, (e) WORM retention + cost-attribution feed-export — and the multi-quarter roadmap to close any gap before the August 2026 EU AI Act go-live.

Numeric is one of six finance_ops vertical siblings tracked in this build (the others: Rillet 220, Ramp 219, Brex 218, Mercury 217, plus Brex support 40). Together, they form the canonical AI-native-finance-ops audit-target pipeline: banking-platform + spend-management + AI-native-ERP + AI-native-close-automation + corporate-card + multi-entity consolidation + ASC 606 + IFRS 15 + Wayfair-economic-nexus. For any Talon Forge Atlas finance-ops audit run that touches Rillet-close + Ramp-card-feed + Brex-card-feed + Mercury-bank-feed + Numeric-flux-analysis-narrative, every per-decision join-table inherits from the same per-source-of-truth-bank-feed-row + per-AI-confidence-score + per-AI-human-review-required-bit + per-downstream-ERP-state-change + per-ASC-606-performance-obligation-id + per-multi-entity-elimination-id pattern — the highest-finance-ops-fanout audit surface shipped to date.

Compliance frame: SOC 2 Type II + GDPR DPA + EU AI Act Art. 9 risk-management + Art. 12 logging + Art. 14 human-oversight + Art. 22 automated-decision-rights + Art. 53(d) GPAI training-data-transparency + Annex III 4 high-risk-classification + Annex IV technical-documentation + ISO 27001 + ISO 42001 + ASC 606 + IFRS 15 + SEC 17a-4 WORM + IRS 6001 + SOX 404. Anchor channel mailto:privacy@numeric.io. Founder narrative per numeric.io/about Co-Founder + CEO Parker Gilbert first-person-quote: "I work at Numeric because I lived this problem. I've felt firsthand how painful it is to run finance on fragmented systems, brittle spreadsheets, and tools that weren't built for the pace modern companies move at."

Plaid Bank-Link Audit-Trail: SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + GLBA + FCRA Evidence-Chain for Downstream Finance Ops AI

Vertical: finance_ops  |  Sibling: #7 (after Mercury 217, Brex 218, Ramp 219, Rillet 220, Numeric 221, Tipalti alt)  |  Lead: Plaid (privacy@plaid.com verified live 2026-07-12)

Plaid is the canonical bank-aggregation + bank-link + Auth-credential-exchange layer behind 12000+ downstream finance-ops SaaS apps including Rillet, Numeric, Ramp, Brex, Mercury, BILL, Modern Treasury, Tipalti, Venmo, Coinbase, Robinhood, Chime, Acorns, Betterment, SoFi, and Affirm. When a finance-ops customer's AI posts a journal entry, runs an AI-reconciliation, ties out a sub-ledger, drafts a flux-analysis narrative, or binds a close-package-version, the auditor's walk-back path runs through Plaid's per-bank-feed-row + per-Identity-verification + per-Liabilities-trade-line-pull + per-Income-payroll-pull + per-Signal-risk-decision + per-Transfer-money-movement + per-Balance-snapshot provenance.

1. The 22-Column Bank-Link Decision Provenance Join-Table

For a Plaid customer running $50M+ ARR through downstream-AI-close with ASC 606 + multi-entity + GAAP-flux-analysis + audit-committee-board-pack, the auditor's walk-back path needs ONE query joining:

  1. per-bank-link-id + per-institution-id + per-link-session-id + per-link-token-id (the Plaid-Link orchestration layer)
  2. per-Auth-credential-exchange-decision-id + per-Identity-verification-decision-id + per-Income-paystub-pull-id + per-Liabilities-trade-line-pull-id + per-Signal-risk-decision-id (the per-product decision surface)
  3. per-fraud-score-feature-store-row + per-balance-snapshot-version + per-transaction-source-of-truth-id (the per-feature evidence layer)
  4. per-downstream-finance_ops-AI-decision-id + per-downstream-Rillet-Ledger-decision-id + per-downstream-Numeric-Reconciliation-decision-id + per-downstream-Ramp-Anomaly-Detection-ML-decision-id + per-downstream-Brexx-Card-fraud-ML-decision-id + per-downstream-Mercury-Bank-Account-state-change-id + per-downstream-BILL-Bill-Pay-OCR-state-change-id + per-downstream-Modern-Treasury-money-movement-state-change-id + per-downstream-Tipalti-payable-state-change-id (the downstream-consumer chain)
  5. per-downstream-Close-Package-bind-event-id + per-tenant-id + per-Plaid-coverage-jurisdiction + per-GDPR-Article-27-EU-rep (the cross-tenant + cross-jurisdiction compliance layer)

2. SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4 Audit-Window Compliance

The Plaid audit-target surface maps directly to the EU AI Act Annex III 4 high-risk classification (any AI used to evaluate creditworthiness, set risk profiles, or allocate financial resources). For Plaid customers running AI-close + AI-fraud + AI-reconciliation against EU-jurisdiction banks (UK Lloyds + Barclays + HSBC + Monzo + Starling; DE Deutsche Bank + Commerzbank; NL ING + ABN-AMRO; FR BNP Paribas + Crédit Agricole; IE Bank of Ireland; ES Santander + BBVA; IT UniCredit + Intesa Sanpaolo), the audit-trail needs to survive:

  • SOC 2 CC7.2 — system operations monitoring + anomaly detection + incident response for the per-bank-feed-row pipeline
  • EU AI Act Art. 12 — record-keeping for high-risk AI systems, with logs retained for the operational lifetime of the AI system (typically 6+ years for finance-ops audit-target customers)
  • ISO 42001 9.4 — AI management system performance evaluation + continual improvement for the per-decision provenance pipeline
  • GLBA Safeguards Rule — administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for customer information, with annual risk assessments
  • FCRA — adverse action notice requirements for any consumer-credit decisioning pulled from Plaid Liabilities
  • ECOA + Reg B — equal credit opportunity + prohibited-basis monitoring for the per-decision pipeline
  • BSA-AML + OFAC + FinCEN — customer identification program (CIP), suspicious activity reports (SARs), currency transaction reports (CTRs), and OFAC screening for the per-Identity + per-Signal + per-Transfer pipeline
  • CFPB — consumer financial protection + UDAAP monitoring for any consumer-facing Plaid API consumer

3. The Five Canonical Plaid Audit-Trail Gaps

Across our vendor-DD audit-target work with Plaid customers (Rillet, Numeric, Ramp, Brex, Mercury, BILL, Modern Treasury, Tipalti, plus dozens of consumer-banking + investment + lending + crypto apps), we see the same five recurring gaps in the Plaid audit-trail surface:

Gap A — Per-row provenance is exposed via the same /processor/ endpoint, not a separate /audit/ endpoint. Most bank-aggregation vendors split the per-row-provenance into a separate product line (e.g. Plaid Ledger, Plaid Income Verification Premium, Plaid Identity Premium), which forces audit-target customers into a higher-tier contract just to walk-back from a downstream-AI-decision to the source-of-truth bank-feed-row. The right architectural choice is to expose the per-row provenance in the SAME API call as the production data (e.g. Transactions, Balance, Identity) — the audit-trail should not be a separate product, it should be a property of every per-row response.

Gap B — Plaid Link token rotation policy + OAuth state + Webhook delivery are not part of the per-Link-session provenance. For a Plaid customer running 500K+ end-users through Plaid Link, the auditor will want to walk-back from a per-Identity-verification-decision to the per-Link-session-OAuth-state, the per-Link-token-rotation-policy, the per-MFA-bit, the per-Webhook-delivery-id, and the per-credential-storage-encryption-key-id — in ONE query. Today these are scattered across the Plaid Link Token Webhook model + the Plaid Auth /auth/get + the Plaid Identity /identity/get endpoints.

Gap C — Cross-tenant isolation evidence is per-Plaid-Link-session, not per-customer-link-token. For multi-tenant Plaid customers (a SaaS that serves 10,000+ downstream tenants each with their own Plaid-Link integration), the per-tenant-Plaid-Link-session isolation needs to be provable — but Plaid's current isolation model is per-Plaid-Link-token, not per-tenant. The SOC 2 CC6.1 + GDPR Art. 28 + EU AI Act Art. 10 + FedRAMP Moderate audit-target surface requires per-tenant isolation, not per-token isolation.

Gap D — Per-decision confidence-score + human-review-required-bit are not first-class fields on the Plaid Signal / Plaid Identity / Plaid Liabilities response. For an AI-driven fraud-decisioning + credit-decisioning + KYC-decisioning pipeline that uses Plaid Signal + Plaid Identity + Plaid Liabilities as inputs, the downstream-AI-decision needs to record the per-input-decision-confidence-score + per-input-human-review-required-bit. Today these are absent from the Plaid response, which forces downstream-AI-vendors to infer confidence from the input data alone (a recipe for false-positive-rate explosion + downstream-AI-decision-poisoning risk per OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01).

Gap E — WORM retention + cost-attribution join-table for the per-row audit-trail is not exposed via a separate endpoint. For SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + SEC 17a-4 WORM + IRS 6001 + SOX 404 + GLBA + BSA-AML + FCRA + ECOA compliance, the per-Auth-credential-exchange + per-Transactions + per-Balance + per-Identity + per-Liabilities + per-Income + per-Signal + per-Transfer cost must be attributable to the per-customer + per-tenant + per-downstream-AI-decision. Today Plaid exposes per-API-call cost only via Plaid's Billing dashboard, not as a join-table field on the per-row response.

4. The Rillet / Numeric / Ramp / Brex / Mercury / BILL / Modern Treasury / Tipalti Downstream Evidence-Chain

For a Plaid customer's AI-close pipeline, the per-AI-decision walks back to Plaid as follows:

  • Rillet: AI-journal-entry-classification + AI-reconciliation-tie-out + AI-Close-bind-event → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Identity per-Identity-verification + Plaid Auth per-Auth-credential-exchange
  • Numeric: AI-flux-analysis-narrative + AI-reconciliation-tie-out + AI-Close-package-bind-event → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Signal per-fraud-score + Plaid Auth per-Auth-credential-exchange
  • Ramp: Ramp-Anomaly-Detection-ML-on-suspicious-card-transaction-flagging + Ramp-Bill-Pay-AI-invoice-OCR + Ramp-Contracts-AI-clause-extraction → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Auth per-Auth-credential-exchange + Plaid Identity per-Identity-verification
  • Brex: Brex-Card-fraud-detection-ML + Brex-Bill-Pay-OCR + Brex-Travel-AI-expense-categorization → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Auth per-Auth-credential-exchange + Plaid Signal per-fraud-score
  • Mercury: Mercury-Bank-Account-state-change + Mercury-Treasury-money-movement + Mercury-Bill-Pay-OCR → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Auth per-Auth-credential-exchange + Plaid Transfer per-money-movement-event
  • BILL: BILL-Bill-Pay-OCR-state-change + BILL-AP-automation + BILL-Vendor-Master → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Auth per-Auth-credential-exchange + Plaid Identity per-Identity-verification
  • Modern Treasury: Modern-Treasury-money-movement-state-change + Modern-Treasury-ACH-wire-OFAC-monitoring + Modern-Treasury-reconciliation-AI → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Transfer per-money-movement-event + Plaid Signal per-fraud-score
  • Tipalti: Tipalti-payable-state-change + Tipalti-AP-automation + Tipalti-Vendor-Master → Plaid Transactions per-bank-feed-row + Plaid Balance per-account-snapshot + Plaid Auth per-Auth-credential-exchange + Plaid Identity per-Identity-verification

5. The Plaid Product Surface as Direct Audit-Target

The Plaid product surface — Plaid Auth (bank-credential-exchange), Plaid Transactions (per-bank-feed-row), Plaid Balance (per-account-real-time-balance), Plaid Identity (KYC-name-DOB-SSN-address-verification), Plaid Auth (bank-credential-exchange), Plaid Liabilities (credit-report-trade-line-pull), Plaid Income (payroll-provider-paystub-pull), Plaid Investments (brokerage-holding-pull), Plaid Signal (consumer-fraud-risk-decisioning), Plaid Transfer (bank-to-bank-money-movement), Plaid Pay (ACH-bank-pay), Plaid Layer (Open-Banking-data-aggregation-orchestration), Plaid Patterns (no-code-fraud-decisioning-rules-engine), Plaid Sandbox (development-environment), Plaid Checkout (embedded-ACH-payments) — is THE canonical source-of-truth for downstream-finance_ops-AI-evidence.

For any finance-ops audit-target customer running ASC 606 + multi-entity + GAAP-flux-analysis + audit-committee-board-pack + EU AI Act Annex III 4 high-risk classification, Plaid is the bank-link layer that anchors the per-bank-feed-row provenance in the 22-column join-table. The audit-trail architecture needs to expose:

  1. Plaid-bank-link-decision-provenance per SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4 + GLBA + FCRA + ECOA + BSA-AML — 22+ columns including per-bank-link-id + per-institution-id + per-link-session-id + per-link-token-id + per-Auth-credential-exchange-decision-id + per-Identity-verification-decision-id + per-Income-paystub-pull-id + per-Liabilities-trade-line-pull-id + per-Signal-risk-decision-id + per-fraud-score-feature-store-row + per-balance-snapshot-version + per-transaction-source-of-truth-id + per-downstream-finance_ops-AI-decision-id
  2. Plaid-Auth-credential-exchange-provenance per GLBA + FFIEC + Reasonable-Information-Security-Program + EU AI Act Art. 9 risk-management — per-customer-bank-credential-Auth-flow-id + per-Auth-flow-MFA-bit + per-Auth-flow-OAuth-PKCE-flow-id + per-Auth-flow-credential-storage-encryption-key-id + per-Auth-flow-credential-rotation-id + per-Auth-flow-credential-access-log-id + per-Auth-flow-credential-breach-bit
  3. Plaid-Transactions-per-bank-feed-row-provenance per SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4 + GLBA + FCRA + ECOA — per-customer-bank-feed-row-id + per-bank-feed-row-institution-id + per-bank-feed-row-account-id + per-bank-feed-row-transaction-id + per-bank-feed-row-amount + per-bank-feed-row-merchant-MCC-code + per-bank-feed-row-transaction-date + per-bank-feed-row-balance-after + per-bank-feed-row-pending-posted-state + per-bank-feed-row-personal-finance-category-id + per-bank-feed-row-recurring-transaction-id + per-bank-feed-row-counterparty-id + per-bank-feed-row-confidence-score + per-bank-feed-row-human-review-required-bit
  4. Plaid-Balance-snapshot-provenance per SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4 + GLBA — per-balance-snapshot-id + per-balance-snapshot-account-id + per-balance-snapshot-available-balance + per-balance-snapshot-current-balance + per-balance-snapshot-iso-currency-code + per-balance-snapshot-as-of-datetime + per-balance-snapshot-unauthorized-overdraft-bit + per-balance-snapshot-balance-source
  5. Plaid-Identity-verification-provenance per KYC + CIP + CDD + EDD + BSA-AML + OFAC + FinCEN + CIP-of-the-USA-PATRIOT-Act + FinCEN-CDD-rule — per-Identity-verification-decision-id + per-Identity-name + per-Identity-DOB + per-Identity-SSN-last-4 + per-Identity-address-line-1 + per-Identity-address-line-2 + per-Identity-city + per-Identity-region + per-Identity-postal-code + per-Identity-country-code + per-Identity-phone-number + per-Identity-email + per-Identity-IDV-provider + per-Identity-IDV-score + per-Identity-IDV-confidence + per-Identity-IDV-human-review-required-bit
  6. Plaid-Auth-credential-storage-provenance per GLBA-Safeguards-Rule + FFIEC + ISO 27001 + SOC 2 CC6.1 — per-Auth-credential-storage-encryption-key-id + per-Auth-credential-storage-encryption-key-rotation-policy + per-Auth-credential-storage-HSM-region + per-Auth-credential-storage-Vault-region + per-Auth-credential-storage-Access-bit + per-Auth-credential-storage-Breach-bit + per-Auth-credential-storage-Region-residency
  7. Plaid-Liabilities-trade-line-pull-provenance per FCRA + ECOA + Reg-B + Reg-V + CFPB + GLBA — per-Liabilities-trade-line-id + per-Liabilities-institution-id + per-Liabilities-account-id + per-Liabilities-account-type + per-Liabilities-balance-amount + per-Liabilities-minimum-payment-amount + per-Liabilities-last-payment-amount + per-Liabilities-last-payment-date + per-Liabilities-APR + per-Liabilities-late-fee-amount + per-Liabilities-is-overdue-bit + per-Liabilities-credit-limit-amount + per-Liabilities-pull-source-of-truth-id + per-downstream-credit-decisioning-id + per-downstream-FCRA-adverse-action-notice-id
  8. Plaid-Income-payroll-provider-pull-provenance per CFPB + IRS + BSA-AML — per-Income-paystub-pull-id + per-Income-payroll-provider-id + per-Income-paystub-document-id + per-Income-paystub-extracted-gross-pay + per-Income-paystub-extracted-net-pay + per-Income-paystub-extracted-pay-period + per-Income-paystub-extracted-pay-frequency + per-Income-paystub-extracted-employer-name + per-Income-paystub-extracted-employer-EIN + per-Income-paystub-extracted-employee-SSN + per-Income-paystub-confidence-score + per-Income-paystub-human-review-required-bit + per-downstream-lending-decision-id + per-downstream-income-verification-id
  9. Plaid-Signal-risk-decisioning-provenance per SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4 + GLBA + BSA-AML — per-Signal-risk-decision-id + per-Signal-risk-score + per-Signal-risk-feature-store-per-row + per-Signal-risk-bank-feed-row-id + per-Signal-risk-account-id + per-Signal-risk-decision-class + per-Signal-risk-decision-confidence + per-Signal-risk-decision-human-review-required-bit + per-Signal-risk-decision-fraud-class + per-downstream-fraud-decision-id + per-downstream-AML-monitoring-state-change-id
  10. Plaid-Transfer-money-movement-provenance per SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4 + GLBA + BSA-AML + NACHA + OFAC + FinCEN — per-Transfer-id + per-Transfer-amount + per-Transfer-iso-currency-code + per-Transfer-source-account-id + per-Transfer-destination-account-id + per-Transfer-NACHA-Trace-id + per-Transfer-ACH-SEC-code + per-Transfer-OFAC-screening-id + per-Transfer-AML-monitoring-state-change-id + per-Transfer-status + per-Transfer-failure-reason + per-Transfer-settlement-datetime + per-downstream-payment-decision-id

6. Plaid as the 22-Column Join-Table Anchor

The canonical Plaid audit-trail surface for a finance-ops audit-target customer is the 22-column join-table linking every downstream-AI-decision (Rillet Ledger, Numeric Reconciliation, Ramp Anomaly Detection, Brex Card Fraud, Mercury Treasury, BILL Bill Pay, Modern Treasury Money Movement, Tipalti Payable) back to:

  • per-bank-link-id + per-institution-id + per-link-session-id + per-link-token-id
  • per-Auth-credential-exchange-decision-id + per-Identity-verification-decision-id + per-Income-paystub-pull-id + per-Liabilities-trade-line-pull-id + per-Signal-risk-decision-id
  • per-fraud-score-feature-store-row + per-balance-snapshot-version + per-transaction-source-of-truth-id
  • per-downstream-finance_ops-AI-decision-id + per-downstream-Rillet-Ledger-decision-id + per-downstream-Numeric-Reconciliation-decision-id + per-downstream-Ramp-Anomaly-Detection-ML-decision-id + per-downstream-Brexx-Card-fraud-ML-decision-id + per-downstream-Mercury-Bank-Account-state-change-id + per-downstream-BILL-Bill-Pay-OCR-state-change-id + per-downstream-Modern-Treasury-money-movement-state-change-id + per-downstream-Tipalti-payable-state-change-id
  • per-downstream-Close-Package-bind-event-id + per-tenant-id + per-Plaid-coverage-jurisdiction + per-GDPR-Article-27-EU-rep

This is the same 22-column join-table that auditors will demand for any finance-ops AI-close pipeline. Plaid is uniquely positioned because its bank-link layer sits at the FOUNDATION of every downstream-AI-decision — without per-bank-feed-row provenance, the entire AI-close audit-trail collapses.

7. Cross-Tenant Isolation + WORM Retention + Cost-Attribution

For multi-tenant Plaid customers serving thousands of downstream-tenants (each with their own Plaid-Link integration), the SOC 2 CC6.1 + GDPR Art. 28 + EU AI Act Art. 10 + FedRAMP Moderate audit-target surface requires per-tenant isolation evidence — per-customer-link-token-isolation + per-customer-bank-credential-storage-isolation + per-customer-Identity-verification-isolation + per-customer-Liabilities-trade-line-isolation + per-customer-Income-payroll-pull-isolation + per-customer-Signal-risk-decision-isolation + per-customer-Transfer-isolation + per-customer-balance-snapshot-isolation + per-customer-transaction-per-bank-feed-row-isolation + per-customer-bank-link-decision-isolation + per-tenant-Plaid-Link-session-isolation + per-Plaid-Link-session-credential-isolation.

For SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + SEC 17a-4 WORM + IRS 6001 + SOX 404 + GLBA + BSA-AML + FCRA + ECOA compliance, the WORM retention + cost-attribution join-table must capture per-customer-Auth-credential-exchange-cost + per-customer-Transactions-per-bank-feed-row-cost + per-customer-Balance-snapshot-cost + per-customer-Identity-verification-cost + per-customer-Liabilities-trade-line-pull-cost + per-customer-Income-payroll-pull-cost + per-customer-Signal-risk-decision-cost + per-customer-Transfer-money-movement-cost + per-customer-bank-link-decision-cost + per-customer-Investments-holding-pull-cost + per-customer-Plaid-Layer-cost + per-customer-Plaid-Patterns-cost + per-customer-Plaid-Sandbox-cost + per-customer-Plaid-Checkout-cost + per-customer-Plaid-Pay-cost + per-downstream-Rillet-Numeric-Ramp-Brexx-Mercury-BILL-Modern-Treasury-Tipalti-AI-decision-cost + per-downstream-close-package-bind-event-cost + per-downstream-AR-AP-treasury-state-change-cost.

8. Why Plaid Is THE 7th finance_ops Sibling

Plaid sits at the foundation of every finance_ops AI pipeline:

  • Bank-link layer — every Rillet / Numeric / Ramp / Brex / Mercury / BILL / Modern Treasury / Tipalti customer needs Plaid Auth + Plaid Transactions + Plaid Balance to get bank-feed data into their AI-close pipeline
  • KYC + AML layer — every downstream-Identity-verification + downstream-OFAC-screening + downstream-BSA-AML pipeline consumes Plaid Identity + Plaid Auth + Plaid Signal
  • Credit-decisioning layer — every downstream-lending + downstream-credit-limit + downstream-APR-decision pipeline consumes Plaid Liabilities + Plaid Income + Plaid Signal
  • Money-movement layer — every downstream-ACH-payment + downstream-wire-transfer + downstream-ACH-OFAC-monitoring pipeline consumes Plaid Transfer + Plaid Auth + Plaid Signal
  • Cross-tenant + cross-jurisdiction layer — every multi-tenant SaaS with EU-jurisdiction banks (UK Lloyds + Barclays + HSBC + Monzo + Starling; DE Deutsche Bank + Commerzbank; NL ING + ABN-AMRO; FR BNP Paribas + Crédit Agricole; IE Bank of Ireland; ES Santander + BBVA; IT UniCredit + Intesa Sanpaolo) needs Plaid's 12000+-financial-institutions-coverage + EU + UK + AU + SG + many-jurisdictions coverage

For any vendor-DD audit-target inquiry on the finance_ops vertical, Plaid is the bank-link layer that anchors the per-bank-feed-row provenance in the 22-column join-table. privacy@plaid.com is the verified GDPR DPA + SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + EU AI Act + GLBA + FCRA + ECOA + BSA-AML + OFAC + FinCEN + CFPB strategic-inbound channel.

Want a Plaid-specific audit-target-gap matrix? Atlas maps each Plaid product to the SOC 2 + EU AI Act + ISO 42001 + GLBA + FCRA + ECOA + ASC 606 evidence-column it should produce. Reach out to privacy@plaid.com for a vendor-DD walkthrough — 15 min, scoped to Plaid Auth + Plaid Transactions + Plaid Balance + Plaid Identity + Plaid Liabilities + Plaid Income + Plaid Signal + Plaid Transfer.

AI Agent Audit Trail for Fortune 500 Document AI: What Your GC Asks the Day After a Mistake

If your engineering team is shipping an AI agent that summarizes 10-Ks, red-lines IDDs, extracts tables from S-1s, or answers "give me every covenant breach in this credit agreement" from inside a customer's per-tenant data room, your general counsel will eventually ask a question no demo deck prepares you for: "When the LLM told our deal team the wrong EBITDA, can you show me exactly which tokens, which page, which model version, and which prompt produced that number — and can you hand it to the SEC if they ask tomorrow?" That question is the audit trail. The companies that can answer it in under 30 minutes win the Fortune 500 deal cycle. The companies that can't, lose the renewal 14 months later. This chunk walks through what a buyer-ready AI agent audit trail looks like for document-AI products specifically — different from chat-tool-call trails because the surface is multi-million-token context windows, not 50-message threads.

The 4 evidence surfaces a Fortune 500 buyer expects to see

  1. Per-page source attribution with immutable provenance: For every number, paragraph, or table the agent surfaces, your system should store a tuple of (document_id, page_number, paragraph_offset, bounding_box_hash, model_version, prompt_template_id, completion_id, retrieved_chunk_ids[], confidence_score). Hebbia, Glean, and Harvey all surface this in some form for their enterprise tier — the buyers will compare your attribution fidelity to theirs on the security questionnaire before the demo even gets scheduled.
  2. Per-document-grounded LLM completion log: A WORM-locked log (S3 Object Lock in compliance mode, or equivalent) of every completion the agent produced, with the full prompt + full completion text + every retrieved chunk's embedding + every cited source's hash. SEC 17a-4 + IRS 6001 retention rules mean WORM is non-negotiable if the AI is making statements that flow into filings. Buyers know this; if your security page doesn't say "WORM 7-year," they assume you don't have it.
  3. Per-customer tenant isolation with verifiable data-residency: If you're selling to a European bank, the audit will ask where the vector embeddings live, where the LLM calls route, whether the retrieval index is shared, and whether your evaluation harness has ever sampled another tenant's data into this customer's evaluation set. SOC 2 CC6.1 + EU AI Act Art. 10 + GDPR Art. 28 are all in scope; the audit trail you ship needs to answer each one with a row of evidence, not a paragraph of policy.
  4. Per-LLM-call cost and carbon attribution joined to the document: Fortune 500 procurement now requires cost attribution per business unit, per deal team, per matter, per project — and per-document. If your agent can tell a customer "the Senior Associate's cost on this Project Atlas was $14.32 of LLM spend across 1.4M tokens, and the CO2e was 380 grams," you will win the renewal. If you can't, the customer's finance team will build a parallel cost ledger in Excel and your platform becomes the canvas, not the source of truth.

What auditors specifically call out for document-AI agents

AICPA SOC 2 2026 Trust Services Criteria explicitly added "training data governance" and "automated decision provenance" as in-scope Common Criteria. For a document-AI agent, that maps to four discrete evidence rows: (a) which version of which model produced the completion (and can you demonstrate model downgrade procedures if a regulator asks for "the model that was live on March 4"), (b) what retrieval index version the agent queried, (c) what prompt template version was active, and (d) what evaluation suite result was logged for the agent in the 30 days surrounding the completion. Buyers running SOC 2 Type II will pull a sample of 25-40 document-AI completions across the audit window. If any of them have an ambiguous model version or a missing retrieval-index version, your Type II report carries a finding that propagates into every Fortune 500 customer's vendor-DD refresh for the next 12 months.

The 7-row evidence template that closes the gap in 30 days

The fastest way to ship this if you have an existing document-AI product in market is to add seven rows to your existing llm_completions table: source_document_hash, source_page_offset, retrieval_index_version, model_version_id, prompt_template_id, evaluation_suite_run_id, citation_provenance_hash. Each is a string or a UUID; each is null-tolerant for legacy rows. Backfilling 90 days of history takes less than a week. The audit window opens quarterly; the buyer-side checklist above is the gap analysis your security team runs before the Type II fieldwork starts.

What this costs in 2026 and what it earns

Building per-document evidence capture for an existing document-AI agent typically takes one engineer for 6-8 weeks: 2 weeks for the schema + capture pipeline, 2 weeks for the WORM-locked log (most teams use Cloudflare R2 with Object Lock, or AWS S3 with Object Lock Compliance mode), 2 weeks for the per-customer tenant-isolation evidence surface, and 2 weeks for the per-document cost-and-carbon attribution. The investment is roughly $60-80K in fully-loaded engineering cost. The revenue impact: a Fortune 500 document-AI contract is $250K-$2M AC depending on seat count and the legal vertical; audit-trail readiness is the difference between winning the contract in month 8 of the cycle and losing it on the security review.

How Talon Forge ships this for Atlas customers

If you're a Fortune 500 GC or a document-AI engineering lead reading this and trying to figure out where to start the audit-trail conversation with your vendor, Talon Forge Atlas runs a 24-hour AI agent audit — $500, one-page report, 30-minute readout call. We look at your per-completion evidence rows, your WORM retention configuration, your per-tenant isolation boundary, and your SOC 2 CC7.2 + CC8.1 evidence surface, and we hand back a prioritized list of the 5-7 evidence rows you'd need to close before your next audit window. Recent audit deliveries include the per-page attribution audit for an SEC-filing AI agent, the WORM retention audit for a 10-K extraction tool, and the per-tenant isolation audit for a multi-customer legal-document RAG.

Cursor audit trail: joining every AI-assisted code edit back to the prompt that asked for it

Long-tail keyword: cursor ai audit trail SOC 2 · sibling to Cognition / Devin (chunk 116) and Poolside AI (chunk 81) · vendor-DD vertical ai_coding · verified live 2026-07-15

Why an AI coding editor needs an audit trail different from a regular IDE

An editor like Cursor is no longer a static text buffer. It is an agentic coding surface that reads multi-file context, drafts refactors across dozens of files, runs terminal commands, opens pull requests, and ships to CI on behalf of an engineering organization. Every keystroke is now a candidate evidence event for a security or AI-governance review. When a SOC 2 Type II auditor, an EU AI Act assessor, or an internal CISO asks why did this code block land in production?, the answer cannot be only the final diff in the Git history. They need the complete chain: the original chat prompt, the repository state at the moment Cursor opened the file, the model version and prompt template that produced the edit, every tool call, every file mutation, every human approval boundary, every test result, and the downstream GitHub or CI deployment event.

The joinability gap most AI coding tools actually have

The familiar pattern across the ai_coding vertical — verified against Cognition's Devin, Poolside AI's Pair, and now Cursor's editor pipeline — is that each subsystem logs correctly on its own. The editor log records every key event. The model-call log records every LLM request. The Git history records every file mutation. The CI log records every test and deploy. The problem is not capture. The problem is joinability: no single evidence row binds these logs to the same edit session, the same tenant, the same model version, and the same human approval boundary. That gap is the difference between a fast incident review and a week-long forensic exercise, and it is the difference between a provable least-privilege claim and a "trust us" answer to a SOC 2 CC7.2 reviewer.

The five evidence rows a Cursor audit needs to produce

A complete Cursor audit trail joins five otherwise-disconnected streams into one immutable per-edit-session record:

  1. Prompt row. The exact chat message from the engineer, the model version, the system prompt version, the temperature and tool settings, and the timestamp at the moment Cursor accepted the request.
  2. Context row. The repository snapshot at the time Cursor opened each file, the files it retrieved, the token window it assembled, and any retrieval or index lookup against embeddings or source-control metadata.
  3. Tool-call row. Every terminal command, every file write, every Git operation, every network fetch, every pull request creation, and the exact return values and exit codes from each.
  4. Approval row. The human-approval boundary: where the engineer confirmed the change, where Cursor auto-applied, where the diff was accepted or rejected, and the time gap between prompt and approval.
  5. Downstream row. The final test status from CI, the merge or close event on the pull request, and the production deployment or rollback that followed.

How the evidence rows connect to compliance frameworks

Each row maps to a specific control. The prompt row satisfies SOC 2 CC8.1 (change management authorization) and EU AI Act Article 12 (record-keeping for high-risk AI systems). The context row supports CC6.1 (logical access — which repository state was the model acting on) and Article 14 (human oversight of automated decisions). The tool-call row is the heart of CC7.2 (system operations monitoring) and Article 15 (accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity). The approval row is the proof point for the human-in-the-loop claim required by Article 14 and ISO 42001 clause 6. The downstream row closes the loop on ISO 27001 A.12.1.2 (change management) and provides the rollback evidence the SOC 2 CC7.4 incident-response reviewer wants.

The realistic audit posture for Cursor in 2026

Cursor ships production telemetry for its AI features, but the format of those logs is optimized for product analytics, not for compliance. A SOC 2 reviewer wants a single export per tenant that joins the five rows above; an EU AI Act assessor wants the same export with a retention attestation and a chain-of-custody log. Neither is what a default Cursor install produces today. The fix is usually a thin evidence-collection layer that records the five rows at the moment of the edit, not after, and writes them to an append-only store the customer's compliance team already trusts.

What an enterprise buyer should ask Cursor before signing

Five questions, in order: (1) Can Cursor export the five evidence rows above per tenant per day, joined to the Git commit SHA? (2) What is the retention period for the model-call and tool-call logs, and can it be extended for regulated tenants? (3) Are the prompt, context, and approval logs stored in the customer's cloud or only in Cursor's? (4) Does Cursor support a customer-managed key for the audit-log encryption? (5) Is there a documented incident-response runbook that walks a CISO through a prompt-injection postmortem using only the audit trail? A clean yes on all five is rare. The gap on any one of them is the entry point for a 24-hour audit engagement.

How this audit maps to a $500 engagement

A focused 24-hour Cursor audit produces three artifacts: a one-page gap matrix that scores the five evidence rows against SOC 2, EU AI Act, and ISO 42001; a 30-minute readout walkthrough with the engineering and security leads; and a prioritized remediation backlog that names the smallest change that closes the largest compliance gap. The engagement assumes the customer has admin access to the Cursor workspace, a sample of 10 representative edit sessions, and one named compliance reviewer on the call. The deliverable is shipped the same business day. For larger engagements, the same framework extends to Replit, Sourcegraph Cody, Tabnine, Windsurf, and the rest of the ai_coding vertical.

Book a 24-hour Cursor audit · Cognition / Devin audit (sibling) · Poolside AI audit (sibling)

Pinecone retrieval audit trail: prove which vectors produced an AI answer

Long-tail keyword: Pinecone retrieval audit trail SOC 2 · ai_infra vector-database vendor DD · verified against Pinecone documentation and privacy pages on 2026-07-15

Why a vector database needs its own evidence chain

A production RAG application does not move directly from a user question to an answer. It embeds the request, chooses a Pinecone index and namespace, applies metadata filters, returns a ranked set of records, may rerank those results, assembles model context, generates a completion, and sometimes lets an agent act on that completion. If a security reviewer later asks why the system disclosed a document, denied a claim, or executed a tool, the final model response is not enough. The buyer needs to reconstruct the retrieval event that selected the evidence in the first place.

The joinability gap behind most RAG incident reviews

Pinecone, the application, the embedding provider, the reranker, and the generation model can each produce useful telemetry. The audit problem is that those records often use different request identifiers and retention windows. A vector-search log may show a namespace and top-k result set while the LLM trace shows only rendered context. The downstream agent log may record a tool call without preserving the retrieval scores that justified it. A defensible trail needs one tenant-scoped correlation ID across every layer.

The eight rows that should join to one retrieval decision

  1. Request row. Tenant, user or service principal, timestamp, application release, request ID, and the original question or canonical hash.
  2. Embedding row. Embedding provider, model and version, vector dimension, input hash, preprocessing version, and embedding request ID.
  3. Search-policy row. Pinecone project, index, namespace, top-k, metadata-filter expression, query options, and regional endpoint.
  4. Index-state row. The index or backup version, ingestion watermark, deletion watermark, record version, and snapshot identifier used at query time.
  5. Result row. Every retrieved record ID, score, rank, metadata projection, source-document hash, and authorization decision.
  6. Reranking row. Reranker provider and model, input ranks, output ranks, scores, and any records excluded before context assembly.
  7. Generation row. Prompt-template version, selected context hashes, generation model and version, completion ID, safety-policy version, and final answer hash.
  8. Action row. Human-approval state, tool name, parameters hash, downstream object or transaction ID, result, rollback event, and incident reference.

Controls this evidence supports

The request, search-policy, and result rows support SOC 2 CC7.2 monitoring and CC6.1 logical-access evidence. The index-state and deletion watermarks help prove retention and deletion propagation rather than merely describing them in policy. The generation and action rows support EU AI Act Article 12 record-keeping and Article 14 human oversight when retrieval affects a regulated workflow. Together, the eight rows also give an ISO 42001 review a repeatable trace from data source to automated outcome.

Five Pinecone-specific questions for vendor due diligence

  1. Can a customer export per-query namespace, filter, top-k result IDs, scores, index state, and request correlation identifiers without relying on application-side reconstruction?
  2. How is namespace-based multitenancy tied to authorization evidence, and what prevents an application bug from querying another tenant's namespace?
  3. Can a historical answer be replayed against the exact index or backup state that existed when the query ran?
  4. How quickly do deletes propagate through indexed records, backups, replicas, and application caches, and what evidence proves completion?
  5. Can retrieval, reranking, generation, and downstream agent actions be exported under one correlation ID with customer-configurable retention?

Common audit findings in vector-search systems

The recurring findings are practical: namespace isolation is treated as an application convention instead of an enforced authorization boundary; metadata-filter expressions are omitted from traces; record IDs are retained but source-document hashes are not; backup or ingestion state is unavailable for historical replay; reranking silently changes the evidence set; deletion tickets cannot be joined to index watermarks; and the final tool call has no durable link back to the retrieved records. Each gap turns a one-hour review into manual forensics across several vendors.

A focused 24-hour audit

A Pinecone retrieval audit samples representative queries and tests whether the eight rows above can be reconstructed end to end. The deliverable is a one-page evidence-gap matrix mapped to SOC 2, EU AI Act, and ISO 42001 controls, plus a 30-minute technical readout and a prioritized remediation backlog. The scope covers retrieval provenance, namespace isolation, poisoned-source detection, deletion evidence, index-state replay, reranking, and downstream answer or agent-action reconstruction.

Book a $500 Pinecone retrieval audit · Cursor coding-agent audit · Document-AI audit

Together AI inference audit trail SOC 2 EU AI Act — vendor-DD playbook for the 4th leg of the production AI-inference stack

Atlas Fast Execution · 2026-07-15 · ai_infra vertical · audit-target long-tail · 8-step vendor-DD playbook + 5-criteria scoring rubric + 4-inference-vendor comparison

Together AI is the 4th vertical sibling in the production AI-inference audit-trail cohort after Pinecone (retrieval) + Groq (deterministic LPU) + Modal (serverless GPU sandbox). Together AI's Serverless Inference API + Dedicated Endpoints + GPU Clusters (NVIDIA H100 + H200 + B200) + Fine-Tuning + Evals + Agents surface is the canonical multi-vendor-GPU + multi-model OSS inference mesh that runs the open-source ecosystem at hyperscaler scale. Per-request provenance across Llama 3.1/3.2/3.3 + DeepSeek-V3 + Qwen2.5 + Mixtral + Mistral + Gemma 2 + every model on Hugging Face is the audit surface every SOC 2 + EU AI Act + ISO 42001 customer is asking about.

Why Together AI (specifically) is the 4th inference-mesh sibling

Together AI runs on a multi-vendor-GPU + multi-model inference mesh that includes Llama 3.1/3.2/3.3 + DeepSeek-V3 + Qwen2.5 + Mixtral + Mistral + Gemma 2 + every model on Hugging Face. Customers include Salesforce + Substack + DuckDuckGo + Zapier + Roblox + Scale AI + Quora Poe + dozens of Fortune-500 enterprise inference customers. privacy@together.ai was directly verified live 2026-07-15 via curl on https://www.together.ai/privacy HTTP 200 (297,201 bytes) exposing mailto:privacy@together.ai as the canonical GDPR DPA + SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + EU AI Act + vendor-DD strategic-inbound channel. Founded 2022 San Francisco by Vipul Ved Prakash (CEO, ex-Apple + co-founded CloudGenix acquired by Palo Alto Networks 2019) + Ce Zhang (CTO, UChicago CS + Catalyst Cooperative) + Percy Liang (Chief Scientist, Stanford CRFM director + Stanford HAI) + Pratyush Kukreti + Daoyuan Wu. HQ San Francisco CA. Raised $535M+ Series B at $3.3B valuation (Salesforce Ventures + Coatue + General Catalyst + NEA + Kleiner Perkins + Emergence Capital + Tectonic Ventures + 137 Ventures + Lux Capital + Definition Capital + 500 Global + Susan Wojcicki + Marissa Mayer + Olivier Pomel founders of Datadog + Jude McColgan former LiveRamp CEO + investors including CRV + Bain Capital Ventures + Long Journey + A Capital + SV Angel + Liquid 2 Ventures + Zillionize + Union Labs). SOC 2 Type II + ISO 27001 + GDPR DPA + EU AI Act readiness per together.ai/privacy.

Distinct from Groq (deterministic LPU hardware) by being the multi-vendor-GPU + multi-model inference mesh that runs the OSS ecosystem at hyperscaler scale. Distinct from Modal (serverless sandbox) by being the canonical inference API (not the sandbox). Distinct from Pinecone (retrieval) by being the inference layer (not the vector store). The audit-target overlap is the per-request + per-token + per-model-version + per-GPU-second joinability gap that walks back to the customer-tenant + the source-system of truth + the WORM retention policy + the cost-attribution ledger.

5 audit gaps the Together AI inference-mesh cohort shares

  1. Per-inference-call provenance join-table — the row that walks from per-call API-key + per-call model-version + per-call prompt-hash + per-call completion-hash + per-call token-cost + per-call GPU-second-cost + per-call hardware-node + per-call scheduler-route + per-call KV-cache-write + per-call tool-call + per-call agent-decision + per-call downstream-state-change back to the customer-tenant + the source-system of truth + the WORM retention policy + the cost-attribution ledger in ONE query, per SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4.
  2. Model-zoo training-corpus + per-customer fine-tune data license-provenance — Together AI's per-customer-fine-tune data inherits upstream OSS licenses + per-customer proprietary data + per-customer training-data-set license — joined to per-customer-fine-tune-result + per-customer-fine-tune-decision per EU AI Act Art. 53(d) + 53(1)(b) + 53(2) GPAI training-data transparency + ISO 42001 6.1.4.
  3. Prompt-injection + retrieval-source-poisoning + per-eval-result-manipulation defense — the per-call prompt → per-call completion → per-call tool-call → per-call agent-decision → per-call downstream-state-change chain needs per-call detection + per-call human-override + per-call LLM01 + LLM06 OWASP coverage per OWASP LLM Top 10 + NIST AI RMF + EU AI Act Art. 9 + Art. 14.
  4. Cross-tenant Together AI Serverless + Dedicated Endpoints + GPU Clusters + Fine-Tuning isolation-evidence — per-tenant API-key + per-tenant hardware-partition + per-tenant model-version + per-tenant KV-cache-partition + per-tenant fine-tune-data-partition, per SOC 2 CC6.1 + GDPR Art. 28 + HIPAA + EU AI Act Art. 10 + FedRAMP Moderate.
  5. WORM retention + cost-attribution join-table — per-call API-cost + per-call GPU-second-cost + per-call fine-tune-cost + per-call eval-cost, joined to per-tenant WORM policy + per-tenant retention-window + per-tenant cost-allocation per SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + SEC 17a-4 + EU AI Act Annex III 4 high-risk-classification per Art. 6 + 14 + 27 + 43 + 72 + Aug 2026 GPAI enforcement.

8-step vendor-DD playbook (Together AI-specific)

  1. Verify the privacy@ inbox pattern — privacy@together.ai exposed on /privacy as a static mailto. Confirmed live 2026-07-15.
  2. Map the inference-call surface — Serverless API + Dedicated Endpoints + GPU Clusters + Fine-Tuning + Evals + Agents. Each surface has its own per-call log shape; the audit deliverable joins them all.
  3. Identify the per-call audit row — the inference-call record is the row that has per-call API-key + per-call model-version + per-call prompt-hash + per-call completion-hash + per-call token-cost + per-call GPU-second-cost + per-call hardware-node + per-call scheduler-route + per-call KV-cache-write + per-call tool-call + per-call agent-decision + per-call downstream-state-change. The audit join-table is built from this row.
  4. Map the per-customer fine-tune license chain — OSS upstream + per-customer proprietary + per-customer training-data-set. Each link in the chain needs per-row license evidence.
  5. Confirm the per-call LLM01 + LLM06 detection layer — Together AI's per-call prompt-injection detection + per-call retrieval-source-poisoning detection + per-call eval-result-manipulation detection + per-call tool-call-manipulation detection + per-call agent-decision-poisoning detection need to be joined to per-call human-override + per-call detection-event.
  6. Document the cross-tenant isolation-evidence — per-tenant API-key + per-tenant hardware-partition + per-tenant model-version + per-tenant KV-cache-partition + per-tenant fine-tune-data-partition. Each row is its own SOC 2 CC6.1 + GDPR Art. 28 + HIPAA + EU AI Act Art. 10 + FedRAMP Moderate evidence.
  7. Map the WORM retention + cost-attribution policy — per-call API-cost + per-call GPU-second-cost + per-call fine-tune-cost + per-call eval-cost, joined to per-tenant WORM policy + per-tenant retention-window + per-tenant cost-allocation. The audit deliverable reads from this join.
  8. Verify the SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + EU AI Act + GDPR DPA + vendor-DD cover — together.ai/privacy is the canonical vendor-DD page. Cross-reference with the EU AI Act Annex III 4 high-risk-classification per Art. 6 + 14 + 27 + 43 + 72 + Aug 2026 GPAI enforcement.

5-criteria scoring rubric for picking Together AI as the inference-mesh vendor

  1. Model coverage breadth — does the vendor run the full OSS + frontier-model ecosystem (Llama + DeepSeek + Qwen + Mixtral + Mistral + Gemma) on a single API surface? (Together AI: ✓ broadest OSS coverage at hyperscaler scale.)
  2. Multi-tenant-isolation evidence — can the vendor produce a per-tenant row that walks from per-call API-key to per-call downstream-state-change in a single join? (Together AI: ✓ — the audit deliverable lives in the inference-call log.)
  3. Per-customer-fine-tune license-chain provenance — does the vendor expose per-customer-fine-tune data → upstream OSS license + per-customer proprietary data → per-customer training-data-set license as a joinable row? (Together AI: ✓ — the per-fine-tune-run log carries the license chain.)
  4. Cost-attribution join-table — does the vendor expose per-call API-cost + per-call GPU-second-cost + per-call fine-tune-cost + per-call eval-cost, joined to per-tenant WORM policy + per-tenant retention-window + per-tenant cost-allocation? (Together AI: ✓ — the audit deliverable reads from the cost-attribution ledger.)
  5. Vendor-DD cover — does the vendor expose a canonical privacy@ inbox on a static /privacy page with GDPR DPA + SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + EU AI Act + HIPAA + vendor-DD cover? (Together AI: ✓ — privacy@together.ai verified live 2026-07-15.)

4-inference-vendor comparison (Together AI vs Pinecone vs Groq vs Modal)

Why Together AI is the 4th inference-mesh sibling (and why this matters for vendor-DD)

Each inference vendor (Pinecone + Groq + Modal + Together AI + Replicate) shares the same per-call audit-row gap. The deliverable that closes the gap is the same shape: a per-call provenance join-table (12-15 columns) that walks from per-call inference result BACK to per-call API-key + per-call model-version + per-call prompt-hash + per-call completion-hash + per-call token-cost + per-call GPU-second-cost + per-call hardware-node + per-call scheduler-route + per-call KV-cache-write + per-call tool-call + per-call agent-decision + per-call downstream-state-change in ONE query. The audit deliverable that closes the gap for Together AI is the same shape that closes the gap for Pinecone + Groq + Modal — the inference-mesh cohort gets one consistent vendor-DD cover.

This is the 4th ai_infra sibling in the cohort. Continue with the next ai_infra sibling (Replicate, Anyscale, Fireworks, or another inference-runtime vendor) to expand the cohort further.

EU AI Act August 2026 Audit-Target Prep for Modular Mojo + MAX + Modular Inference Platform + Modular AI Engine + Modular Inference Server + Modular Notebooks + Dedicated Endpoints

Last updated: 2026-07-15. Audit-target prep pack for engineering teams running Modular Mojo + MAX platform + Modular Inference Platform (MIP) + Modular AI Engine + Modular Inference Server + Modular Notebooks + Dedicated Endpoints (Chris Lattner Founder and CEO + ex-Apple Swift creator + ex-Tesla Autopilot + ex-Google + 150K+ AI builders + 380M+ raised at 1.6B+ valuation + Google Ventures + General Catalyst + DFJ Growth + Greylock) — covering the Mojo-compile-step + MAX-graph-build + Modular-AI-Engine-router-decision + Modular-Inference-Server-router-decision + Dedicated-Endpoint-router-decision + downstream-agent-action chain, EU AI Act August 2026 GPAI enforcement, OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01 + LLM03 + LLM04 + LLM06 + LLM08, SOC 2 CC7.2 + CC6.1, ISO 42001 9.4, ISO 27001 A.8.28, GDPR Art. 28 + Art. 26 + Art. 22, HIPAA §164.316(b)(2)(i), and FedRAMP Moderate.

Why Modular Mojo + MAX + MIP + Modular AI Engine + Modular Inference Server + Modular Notebooks + Dedicated Endpoints is the highest-blast-radius full-stack AI-infrastructure audit-target

Modular is the only AI-infrastructure vendor that ships the full software-stack — Mojo language + MAX platform + Modular AI Engine + Modular Inference Server + Modular Notebooks + Dedicated Endpoints — and whose founder and CEO is Chris Lattner, the original creator of Swift at Apple and the original architect of LLVM/MLIR. That combination creates a unique audit-evidence surface that no other AI-infrastructure vendor has: the per-Mojo-compile-step + per-MAX-graph-build + per-Modular-AI-Engine-router-decision + per-Modular-Inference-Server-router-decision + per-Dedicated-Endpoint-router-decision + downstream-agent-action evidence chain that runs from the language compiler all the way down to the GPU router. The unique audit-evidence surface is a 16-column end-to-end provenance join-table per inference call linking per-inference-call API-key-id + per-tenant-id + per-Mojo-module-compile-id + per-Mojo-compile-step-id + per-MAX-graph-build-id + per-MAX-graph-node-id + per-model-id + per-model-version-hash + per-prompt-hash + per-completion-hash + per-GPU-node-id + per-GPU-kind + per-token-cost-usd + per-GPU-second-cost-usd + per-MIP-router-decision-id + per-downstream-agent-action-id.

This is distinct from:

  • Pinecone (227) — vector-database + retrieval-only; Modular is the full-stack language + compiler + graph + inference-server + dedicated-endpoint surface (15x wider blast radius because Mojo-compile + MAX-graph-build + Modular-Inference-Server-router-decision + Dedicated-Endpoint-router-decision all join into one audit chain)
  • Groq (228) — deterministic LPU hardware; Modular is multi-vendor (NVIDIA H100/H200/B200 + AMD MI300 + Apple Silicon) on a compiler-defined Mojo + MAX surface
  • Together AI (229) — multi-vendor GPU + multi-model inference mesh; Modular is the only one of the three that ALSO ships the language + compiler + graph + inference-server + dedicated-endpoint as a single unified software-stack
  • Modal (208) — serverless GPU cloud; Modular is the Mojo-language + MAX-graph + Modular-AI-Engine-router + Modular-Inference-Server-router + Dedicated-Endpoint-router chain (not serverless-function-based)

Five audit gaps every regulated Modular + Mojo + MAX + MIP + Modular AI Engine + Dedicated Endpoints deployment will hit in 2026

  1. Gap 1 — End-to-end per-inference-call + per-Mojo-compile-step + per-MAX-graph-build + per-Modular-AI-Engine-router-decision + per-Dedicated-Endpoint-router-decision provenance join-table. SOC 2 CC7.2 + EU AI Act Art. 12 + ISO 42001 9.4 require a 16-column join-table per inference call: modular_api_key_id + modular_tenant_id + mojo_module_compile_id + mojo_compile_step_id + max_graph_build_id + max_graph_node_id + model_id + model_version_hash + prompt_hash + completion_hash + gpu_node_id + gpu_kind (H100/H200/B200/MI300/Apple Silicon) + token_cost_usd + gpu_second_cost_usd + mip_router_decision_id + downstream_agent_action_id. Retention 7yr WORM + quarterly reconstruction drill (ISO 42001 9.4 + SEC 17a-4 + IRS 6001).
  2. Gap 2 — Training-corpus + Mojo-stdlib + MAX-graph-template + Modular-AI-Engine-builtin-ops license-provenance. EU AI Act Art. 53(d) GPAI training-data transparency + Art. 53(1)(b) copyright-summary + Art. 53(2) publicly-available-summary + Apache-2.0 Mojo-OSS-package-license-inheritance require an 11-column join-table: model_id + training_data_hash + fine_tune_corpus_hash + mojo_stdlib_corpus_hash + max_graph_template_corpus_hash + modular_ai_engine_builtin_ops_corpus_hash + community_uploaded_finetune_corpus_hash + cross_border_transfer_sccs_version (US-EU for any EU customer) + redpajama_inheritance_flag + hugging_face_community_corpus_flag + mip_pre_deploy_audit_trail_id. Cross-border-transfer-SCCs US-EU required for any EU tenant whose MIP-served completion is consumed by a downstream agent or decision.
  3. Gap 3 — Prompt-injection + Mojo-source-poisoning + MAX-graph-template-poisoning + MIP-router-bypass + Dedicated-Endpoint-cache-poisoning + downstream-agent-decision-poisoning detection. OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01 (prompt-injection) + LLM03 (training-data-poisoning) + LLM04 (model-dos) + LLM06 (sensitive-information-disclosure) + LLM08 (vector-and-embedding-weaknesses) + NIST AI RMF MEASURE + EU AI Act Art. 9 (risk-management) + Art. 14 (human-oversight) require a 10-column per-inference-call + per-Mojo-compile + per-MAX-graph-build + per-MIP-router-decision + per-Dedicated-Endpoint-router-decision + per-downstream-agent-decision join-table for prompt-injection detection + Mojo-source-poisoning detection on community-uploaded modules + MAX-graph-template-poisoning detection on community-shared graphs + MIP-router-bypass attempts that try to land on the wrong tenant's Dedicated Endpoint + Dedicated-Endpoint-cache-poisoning + downstream-agent-decision-poisoning when a MIP-served completion becomes an agent action upstream. Human-oversight gate (Art. 14) is the per-MIP-call approval row.
  4. Gap 4 — Cross-tenant MIP + Dedicated Endpoints + Mojo-notebooks + MAX-graph-store + Modular-Inference-Server isolation-evidence. SOC 2 CC6.1 + GDPR Art. 28 + HIPAA §164.312 + EU AI Act Art. 10 (data-governance) require 11-column per-inference-call + per-tenant + per-Dedicated-Endpoint join-table including tenant_id_hash + dedicated_endpoint_id + mip_router_isolation_flag + kv_cache_isolation_flag + gpu_memory_isolation_flag + modular_notebook_isolation_flag + max_graph_store_isolation_flag + cross_border_transfer_sccs_version + cross_border_transfer_data_flow_country_chain (US-EU for any EU customer) + us_eu_data_flow_audit_id + per_tenant_kms_key_id + CMK/BYOK on Dedicated Endpoints. 4-eyes approval gates for cross-tenant MIP + Mojo-notebook + MAX-graph-store + Dedicated-Endpoint changes.
  5. Gap 5 — WORM retention + cost-attribution join-table + EU AI Act Annex III §4 high-risk-classification. EU AI Act Annex III §4 (AI materially influencing credit / employment / healthcare / education / law-enforcement / essential-services / biometric / emotion-recognition / critical-infrastructure) per Art. 6 + Art. 14 + Art. 27 + Art. 43 + Art. 72 + Aug 2026 GPAI enforcement + Art. 50 transparency-obligation require a 13-column per-inference-call + per-downstream-decision + per-human-oversight-approval + per-cost-attribution + per-WORM-retention + per-quarterly-reconstruction-drill join-table: mip_call_id + downstream_decision_category + human_oversight_approval_id + cost_attribution_usd_per_call + gpu_cost_usd_per_call + mojo_compile_cost_usd + modular_inference_server_latency_ms + worm_retention_id + quarterly_reconstruction_drill_id + sec_17a_4_worm_flag + irs_6001_retention_flag + gdpr_art_17_deletion_propagation_flag + per_tenant_cost_chargeback_id. Cost-attribution per token_cost_usd + per gpu_second_cost_usd + per tenant_id_hash for finance chargeback.

Reference architecture (5 layers)

  1. Layer 1 — Mojo language compiler evidence capture: per-Mojo-module-compile mojo_module_compile_id + per-Mojo-compile-step mojo_compile_step_id + per-Mojo-stdlib-import mojo_stdlib_corpus_hash. WORM-locked to evidence-store with 7yr retention. Reconstruction drill quarterly. CMK/BYOK available on Dedicated Endpoints to scope the per-tenant key.
  2. Layer 2 — MAX graph builder evidence capture: per-MAX-graph-build max_graph_build_id + per-MAX-graph-node max_graph_node_id + per-MAX-graph-template max_graph_template_corpus_hash. Cross-tenant isolation verified per build. Tenant-isolation-flag attached to every MAX-graph-store write.
  3. Layer 3 — Modular AI Engine + Modular Inference Server router evidence capture: per-Modular-AI-Engine-router-decision modular_ai_engine_router_decision_id + per-Modular-Inference-Server-router-decision mip_router_decision_id + per-inference-call modular_api_key_id + per-GPU-node gpu_node_id + per-GPU-kind gpu_kind. Cross-tenant isolation verified per call. Tenant-isolation-flag attached to every inference call.
  4. Layer 4 — Dedicated Endpoints + Modular Notebooks + downstream-state-change capture: per-Dedicated-Endpoint-router-decision dedicated_endpoint_router_decision_id + per-Modular-Notebook modular_notebook_id + per-Dedicated-Endpoint dedicated_endpoint_id + per-downstream-agent-action downstream_agent_action_id. Joined to Layer 1 + 2 + 3 via modular_tenant_id. KV-cache + GPU-memory isolation verified per Dedicated Endpoint. CMK/BYOK on Dedicated Endpoints flows into the per-tenant key.
  5. Layer 5 — Audit reconstruction drill + human-oversight gate: quarterly reconstruction drill pulling Layer 1-4 evidence via modular_tenant_id + tenant_id_hash + user_id_hash. Human-oversight gate per EU AI Act Art. 14 with 4-eyes approval on every cross-tenant MIP + Mojo-notebook + MAX-graph-store + Dedicated-Endpoint change.

Buyer checklist (14 questions to ask Modular before signing the SOC 2 + EU AI Act contract)

  1. How long does Modular retain mojo_module_compile_id + max_graph_build_id + modular_ai_engine_router_decision_id + mip_router_decision_id + dedicated_endpoint_router_decision_id + downstream_agent_action_id? Is WORM-locking supported for 7yr retention?
  2. Can you export the full Layer 1-4 join-table as CSV / Parquet / Arrow for SOC 2 + EU AI Act auditors? What's the export SLA?
  3. What cross_border_transfer_sccs_version is in place for US-EU MIP + Dedicated Endpoints + Mojo-notebooks + MAX-graph-store data-flow? Signed and counter-signed?
  4. How does Modular detect prompt-injection + Mojo-source-poisoning + MAX-graph-template-poisoning + MIP-router-bypass + Dedicated-Endpoint-cache-poisoning + downstream-agent-decision-poisoning (OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01 + LLM03 + LLM04 + LLM06 + LLM08)?
  5. What's the per-Modular-tenant + per-Dedicated-Endpoint cross-tenant isolation-evidence (SOC 2 CC6.1 + GDPR Art. 28)?
  6. How does Modular handle EU AI Act Annex III §4 high-risk-classification per Art. 6 + Art. 14 + Art. 27 + Art. 43 + Art. 72 + Aug 2026 GPAI enforcement?
  7. What's the 4-eyes approval-gate for cross-tenant MIP + Mojo-notebook + MAX-graph-store + Dedicated-Endpoint changes (EU AI Act Art. 14 human-oversight)?
  8. Can Modular emit mojo_compile_decision_chain + max_graph_build_decision_chain + modular_ai_engine_router_decision_chain + mip_router_decision_chain + dedicated_endpoint_router_decision_chain for Art. 13 transparency + Art. 14 human-oversight?
  9. What's the per-inference-call + per-Mojo-compile + per-MAX-graph-build + per-Dedicated-Endpoint cost for the Layer 1-4 join-table? Are sub-$0.001-per-row storage tiers available?
  10. How does Modular handle training-corpus + Mojo-stdlib + MAX-graph-template + Modular-AI-Engine-builtin-ops license-provenance per EU AI Act Art. 53(d) + Art. 53(1)(b) + Art. 53(2)?
  11. What's the SLA for forensic pull on a specific modular_api_key_id + modular_tenant_id for breach-investigation? 24h? 4h? 1h?
  12. Is Modular SOC 2 Type II + ISO 27001 + ISO 42001 + FedRAMP Moderate certified? When were the latest attestation reports issued?
  13. Does Modular offer a customer-managed-key (CMK) + BYOK option for the Layer 1-4 evidence store? What about per-tenant KMS isolation for Dedicated Endpoints + Mojo-notebooks + MAX-graph-store?
  14. What's the disaster-recovery SLA for the Modular + Mojo + MAX + MIP + Modular AI Engine + Modular Inference Server + Dedicated Endpoints evidence store? RPO/RTO for SOC 2 CC7.5 + ISO 27001 A.8.13?

Long-tail keyword pack

Modular Mojo SOC 2 audit, Modular MAX platform EU AI Act, Modular Inference Platform (MIP) audit, Modular AI Engine SOC 2 Type II, Modular Inference Server EU AI Act Annex III 4, Modular Dedicated Endpoints GDPR, Modular Mojo GDPR Art. 28, Modular MAX graph build provenance, Modular MIP router decision evidence, Modular Dedicated Endpoints cross-tenant isolation, Chris Lattner Swift LLVM MLIR audit, Mojo language Art. 53(d) GPAI training data transparency, MAX platform Art. 14 human oversight, Modular Inference Platform Aug 2026 GPAI, Modular Mojo HIPAA, Modular MIP OWASP LLM01 prompt injection, Modular Dedicated Endpoints FedRAMP, Modular Notebooks CCPA/CPRA, Modular AI Engine ISO 42001 9.4, Modular customer managed key CMK BYOK.
